Good morning, everyone. Today I want to talk about something we all take for granted: time management. I know you have heard this advice a thousand times before, but I think most of it shares one common blind spot. It assumes your time is a divisible, schedulable resource. In reality, time behaves more like a river, with its own currents and eddies.

Let me switch gears for a moment. I have been reading a fascinating book about fifth-century Athens. The author describes how citizens would gather in the agora to debate politics. It was a remarkably specific form of public life. Our internet, despite connecting everyone, seems to have made genuine conversation rarer. Everyone speaks, but few listen.

I want to close with a story. A friend of mine is a surgeon. She once told me that the most important thing in surgery is not technique, it is focus. When your attention drifts, no amount of skill can save the patient. I think this principle extends to every part of our lives. Focus is the most precious and scarce resource of our age. Thank you.
